pr.title: Split .data section from code_8008521C into fighters
pr.author: r-burns

summary: Moved the large fighter-related .data section out of asm/melee/ft/code_8008521C.s and into per-character assembly files under asm/melee/ft/chara, with obj_files.mk updated so the new/expanded fighter objects are built. This was mostly a data organization split: code_8008521C.s lost nearly all of its data payload, while files such as ftkirby.s, ftclink.s, ftcrazyhand.s, ftdonkey_2.s, and many other fighter files gained .section .data blocks containing action-state-style tables, asset filename strings, and pointer arrays. There were no PR body notes or review comments in the slice, so intent beyond the title and diff is inferred only from the file movement.
what_changed: Removed 12896 lines from asm/melee/ft/code_8008521C.s while adding only 9 lines there, indicating the old monolithic file was almost entirely drained of this .data content.

Added or expanded per-fighter files in asm/melee/ft/chara for most of the roster, including ftclink.s, ftkirby.s, fticeclimber.s, ftmasterhand.s, ftdonkey_2.s, ftpurin.s, ftcrazyhand.s, ftyoshi.s, ftfox.s, ftness.s, ftkoopa.s, ftfalco.s, ftgamewatch.s, ftemblem.s, ftmars.s, ftseak.s, ftpeach.s, ftlink.s, ftpichu.s, ftpikachu.s, ftfalcon.s, ftluigi.s, ftganon.s, ftzelda.s, ftmewtwo.s, ftsamus.s, ftgigakoopa.s, ftdrmario.s, ftmario.s, ftsandbag.s, ftzakoboy.s, ftzakogirl.s, ftdonkey_1.s, and ftdonkey_2.s.

Created a new ftclink.s file with macros.inc included, a .section .data block, .balign directives, globals such as lbl_803D0FA0, strings such as PlCl.dat and ftDataClink, and pointer arrays for costume/demo asset names.

Inserted large .data blocks into existing fighter assembly files, for example ftcrazyhand.s gained lbl_803D41F8 through asset strings such as PlCh.dat and ftDataCrazyhand, and ftdonkey_2.s gained lbl_803CB838 and related data.
Moved small non-table debug/source strings too: ftdonkey_1.s gained a .data block containing "couldn't get walk frame\n" and "ftwalkcommon.c".
Updated obj_files.mk with 11 added lines so the build knows about the split fighter data objects.
smart_moves: Kept data physically near the fighter-specific assembly it belongs to instead of leaving all fighter .data in the generic code_8008521C.s bucket.

Preserved low-level assembly representation directly: .global labels, .4byte pointer entries, NULL entries, .asciz asset strings, and .balign directives were retained rather than prematurely converting these tables to guessed C structures.
Used per-character file naming under asm/melee/ft/chara, which makes later fighter decompilation and symbol identification more searchable by fighter short name.
Updated the object list alongside the data movement, avoiding a common split-file failure mode where newly created assembly files are not linked into the build.
Maintained alignment boundaries such as .balign 8 at the start of .data and .balign 4 or .balign 8 after strings where shown in the diff excerpt.
decomp_lessons: For large unidentified .data blobs, a useful early step is to split by subsystem or fighter ownership before attempting full type recovery.
Action-state tables and asset-name tables can often be safely moved as assembly data as long as label order, alignment, pointer targets, and NULL slots are preserved exactly.

Per-fighter data files improve later reverse-engineering locality: asset strings like PlCl.dat, ftDataClink, PlyClink5K_Share_joint, PlCh.dat, and ftDataCrazyhand become immediate anchors for identifying the owning fighter module.

When carving data out of a monolithic asm file, build configuration changes are part of the matching tactic; the split only works if obj_files.mk links the new object files in the correct arrangement.

Do not over-name the contents during a mechanical split. This PR kept address-style lbl_ names and existing func_ references, which avoids introducing speculative symbol names while still improving file organization.
naming_conventions: Fighter assembly files use lowercase ft-prefixed character names under asm/melee/ft/chara, such as ftclink.s, ftcrazyhand.s, ftdonkey_2.s, ftpikachu.s, and ftgamewatch.s.
Unidentified data symbols remain address-derived labels such as lbl_803D0FA0, lbl_803D1240, lbl_803D41F8, lbl_803D4818, lbl_803CB838, and lbl_803C70F0.
Unidentified functions remain address-derived func_ names such as func_800CEDE0, func_800CEE00, func_800761C8, func_80149354, and func_800DF9F0.

Asset strings preserve game naming conventions, including short fighter file prefixes like PlCl.dat, PlClNr.dat, PlCh.dat, PlChNr.dat, and descriptive symbols embedded in strings such as ftDataClink and ftDataCrazyhand.
Character variant files and joint strings preserve capitalization and suffixes exactly, for example PlyClink5K_Share_joint, PlyClink5KRe_Share_matanim_joint, and PlyCrazyhand_Share_joint.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used assembly-level .section .data splits rather than C reconstruction, which is appropriate for a mechanical relocation of large data tables.
Preserved .balign 8 at data-section starts and per-string .balign 4 or .balign 8 padding where required.
Kept pointer tables as explicit .4byte entries referencing existing function labels, data labels, or NULL.
Created a new file with .include "macros.inc" for ftclink.s before declaring .section .data, matching the style needed for assembly macros and NULL definitions.
Moved data by fighter ownership while retaining global labels, letting existing relocations continue to resolve after the build-system update.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or review records were present in the provided PR slice.
No explicit reviewer rationale was available; conclusions are based on the PR title, changed-file list, and diff excerpt.
searchable_terms: Split .data section from code_8008521C into fighters
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that convert the per-fighter .data tables from asm/melee/ft/chara/*.s into C structs.
Search for references to lbl_803D0FA0 and lbl_803D41F8 to identify the action-state table type used by fighter data.
Inspect obj_files.mk after PR 329 to understand the exact object ordering required for matching after the split.
Look for subsequent renames of ftclink asset labels such as PlCl.dat and ftDataClink.
Compare PR 329 with other monolithic data-split PRs for common alignment and object-list update patterns.
